title_en: Content and Application of the Maxims
content_id:
Kaidah-kaidah hukum yang dianalisis dalam Al-Mantssur mencakup berbagai topik yang luar biasa dan menunjukkan koherensi yang mendasari apa yang mungkin tampak seperti kumpulan hukum-hukum positif yang sewenang-wenang. Beberapa kelompok kaidah menonjol karena kepentingan teoritisnya dan penerapan praktisnya.

Kaidah-kaidah yang berkaitan dengan niat dan tujuan adalah fondasi analisis hukum Islam. Prinsip bahwa 'urusan-urusan dinilai berdasarkan tujuan-tujuannya' (al-umur bi-maqasidiha) memiliki implikasi dalam hukum ritual (di mana ketulusan ibadah sangat penting), hukum komersial (di mana niat para pihak menentukan keabsahan akad-akad), dan hukum pidana (di mana niat membedakan pembunuhan berencana dari pembunuhan tidak sengaja). Analisis az-Zarkasyi tentang penerapan dan batas-batas prinsip ini termasuk yang paling menyeluruh dalam literatur klasik.

Kaidah-kaidah yang berkaitan dengan kepastian dan keraguan mengatur pengelolaan ketidakpastian hukum di semua domain. Prinsip bahwa 'kepastian tidak ditentukan oleh keraguan' (al-yaqin la yuzal bisy-syakk) berarti bahwa begitu suatu keadaan hukum telah ditetapkan, yaitu seseorang dalam keadaan suci secara ritual, pernikahan sah, harta milik seseorang tertentu, keadaan itu berlanjut sampai suatu keadaan yang pasti dan berlawanan ditetapkan. Prinsip ini mencegah kehati-hatian yang berlebihan membuat praktik keagamaan menjadi tidak mungkin dan memberikan dasar yang stabil bagi presumsi hukum. Az-Zarkasyi memeriksa banyak penerapan prinsip ini dan kasus-kasus di mana prinsip ini harus dikualifikasi.

Kaidah-kaidah yang berkaitan dengan bahaya dan pencegahannya menangani salah satu bidang fikih yang paling penting secara praktis. Prinsip 'bahaya harus dihilangkan' (al-darar yuzal) mendasari mekanisme hukum Islam untuk mencegah cedera, memberi kompensasi kepada mereka yang telah dirugikan, dan membatalkan akad-akad dan pengaturan-pengaturan yang terbukti merugikan salah satu pihak. Analisis az-Zarkasyi menghubungkan prinsip ini dengan hukum jual beli, kontrak sewa, pernikahan, dan kesejahteraan publik dengan cara-cara yang menunjukkan penerapannya yang meresap.

Kaidah-kaidah yang berkaitan dengan adat sangat penting untuk memahami kemampuan adaptasi hukum Islam terhadap konteks sosial yang beragam. Prinsip 'adat adalah otoritatif' (al-'adat muhakkamah) mengakui bahwa banyak penentuan hukum yang tampak spesifik sebenarnya bersifat kontekstual: apa yang dianggap sebagai kehati-hatian yang wajar, kompensasi yang memadai, atau kinerja yang tepat waktu bergantung pada standar adat dari suatu waktu dan tempat tertentu. Pembahasan az-Zarkasyi tentang prinsip ini telah dikutip oleh ulama-ulama kontemporer yang tertarik pada hubungan antara hukum Islam dan konteks sosial.
content_en:
The legal maxims analyzed in Al-Manthur cover a remarkable range of topics and demonstrate the coherence underlying what might otherwise appear to be an arbitrary collection of positive law rules. Several clusters of maxims stand out for their theoretical importance and practical application.

The maxims related to intention and purpose are foundational to Islamic legal analysis. The principle that 'matters are judged by their purposes' (al-umur bi-maqasidiha) has implications across ritual law (where the sincerity of worship is essential), commercial law (where the parties' intentions determine the validity of contracts), and criminal law (where intent distinguishes murder from manslaughter). Az-Zarkashi's analysis of the applications and limits of this principle is among the most thorough in the classical literature.

The maxims related to certainty and doubt govern the management of legal uncertainty across all domains. The principle that 'certainty is not overridden by doubt' (al-yaqin la yuzal bish-shakk) means that once a legal state is established — a person is ritually pure, a marriage is valid, property belongs to a given person — it continues until a certain contrary state is established. This principle prevents excessive caution from making religious practice impossible and provides a stable basis for legal presumptions. Az-Zarkashi examines the many applications of this principle and the cases where it must be qualified.

The maxims related to harm and its prevention address one of the most practically important areas of Islamic jurisprudence. The principle 'harm must be removed' (al-darar yuzal) grounds Islamic law's mechanisms for preventing injury, compensating those who have been harmed, and annulling contracts and arrangements that prove harmful to a party. Az-Zarkashi's analysis connects this principle to the law of sales, rental contracts, marriage, and public welfare in ways that show its pervasive application.

The maxims related to custom are particularly important for understanding the adaptability of Islamic law to diverse social contexts. The principle 'custom is authoritative' (al-'adat muhakkamah) recognizes that many legal determinations that appear specific are actually contextual — what counts as reasonable care, adequate compensation, or timely performance depends on the customary standards of a given time and place. Az-Zarkashi's treatment of this principle has been cited by contemporary scholars interested in the relationship between Islamic law and social context.
content_ar: The legal maxims analyzed in Al-Manthur cover a remarkable range of topics and demonstrate the coherence underlying what might otherwise appear to be an arbitrary collection of positive law rules. Several clusters of maxims stand out for their theoretical importance and practical application. The maxims related to intention and purpose are foundational to Islamic legal analysis. The principle that 'matters are judged by their purposes' (al-umur bi-maqasidiha) has implications across ritual law (where the sincerity of worship is الجوهري), commercial law (where the parties' intentions determine the validity of contracts), and criminal law (where intent distinguishes murder from manslaughter). Az-Zarkashi's analysis of the applications and limits of this principle is among the most thorough in the الكلاسيكي literature. The maxims related to certainty and doubt govern the management of legal uncertainty across all domains. The principle that 'certainty is not overridden by doubt' (al-yaqin la yuzal bish-shakk) means that حين a legal state is established — a person is ritually pure, a marriage is valid, property belongs to a given person — it continues حتى a certain contrary state is established. This principle prevents excessive caution from making religious practice impossible and provides a stable basis for legal presumptions. Az-Zarkashi examines the many applications of this principle and the cases where it must be qualified. The maxims related to harm and its prevention address one of the most practically المهم areas of Islamic jurisprudence. The principle 'harm must be removed' (al-darar yuzal) grounds Islamic law's mechanisms for preventing injury, compensating those who have been harmed, and annulling contracts and arrangements that prove harmful to a party. Az-Zarkashi's analysis connects this principle to the law of sales, rental contracts, marriage, and public welfare in ways that show its pervasive application. The maxims related to custom are particularly المهم for الفهم the adaptability of Islamic law to diverse social contexts. The principle 'custom is المعتمد' (al-'adat muhakkamah) recognizes that many legal determinations that appear specific are actually contextual — what counts as reasonable care, adequate compensation, or timely performance depends on the customary standards of a given time and place. Az-Zarkashi's treatment of this principle has been cited by contemporary العلماء interested in the relationship between Islamic law and social context.
